questions.question_text: Arizona uses a dual assessment system. What are the two assessment values?
questions.correct_answer: Full Cash Value and Limited Property Value
distractors: Market Value and Assessed Value amounts
Tax Value and Sale Value calculations
Primary Value and Secondary Value figures
questions.explanation: Arizona's dual assessment system uses Full Cash Value (market value from the assessor) and Limited Property Value (a capped value that cannot increase more than 5% per year, ARS §42-13301). Since tax year 2015, LPV is the taxable value used to compute all property taxes; FCV serves as the market-value ceiling and is used in valuation appeals.

questions.question_text: The Limited Property Value (LPV) cannot increase by more than what percentage annually?
questions.explanation: LPV cannot increase more than 5% per year, providing homeowners with some predictability in primary tax assessments even when market values rise rapidly. This is less restrictive than California's Prop 13 (2% cap).

questions.question_text: A home's Full Cash Value is $400,000. What is the assessment ratio for residential property in Arizona?
questions.explanation: Residential (class three) property is assessed at 10% of the Limited Property Value (ARS §42-15003). Since tax year 2015, assessed value for all property taxes is computed from LPV — there is no separate FCV-based assessed value.

questions.question_text: Which value does the county assessor determine to represent a property's market value?
questions.explanation: Full Cash Value (FCV) represents the property's market value as determined by the county assessor and is used in valuation appeals. LPV — capped at 5% annual growth — has been the taxable value used to compute all property taxes since tax year 2015.

questions.question_text: A home's LPV was $300,000 last year but the FCV jumped to $400,000. What is the maximum LPV this year?
questions.explanation: LPV can increase by a maximum of 5% per year. Last year's LPV of $300,000 x 1.05 = $315,000 maximum, regardless of how much the FCV increased. This protects homeowners from sudden tax spikes.

questions.question_text: Arizona primary property taxes fund which types of government services?
questions.correct_answer: Schools, counties, cities, and community colleges
questions.explanation: Primary taxes fund the day-to-day operational budgets of schools, counties, cities, and community colleges. Secondary taxes fund voter-approved bonds, overrides, and special districts — including fire districts (ARS §48-807, §48-805.02).

questions.question_text: Secondary property taxes in Arizona are based on which value?
questions.explanation: Since tax year 2015, Proposition 117 (Ariz. Const. art. IX, §18) made Limited Property Value (LPV) the sole taxable value for both primary and secondary property taxes. Full Cash Value now serves only as the ceiling for LPV and for valuation appeals.

questions.question_text: When are Arizona property tax payments due?
questions.correct_answer: October 1 and March 1 in two installments
distractors: January 1 and July 1 in two installments
Quarterly on the first of each quarter
questions.explanation: Arizona property tax bills are due in two installments: the first half by October 1 and the second half by March 1. Late payments are subject to interest and penalties.

questions.question_text: Voter-approved school district bonds are funded through which type of Arizona property tax?
questions.explanation: Voter-approved bonds and overrides are funded through secondary property taxes, while primary taxes fund operational budgets. Since tax year 2015 (Proposition 117), both primary and secondary taxes are levied on Limited Property Value (LPV).

questions.question_text: Which of the following best describes how primary and secondary taxes differ in Arizona?
questions.correct_answer: Primary taxes fund day-to-day operations; secondary taxes fund voter-approved bonds and overrides
distractors: Primary is state; secondary is federal
Primary is for homes; secondary is for commercial
Primary is assessed annually; secondary is biennial
questions.explanation: The distinction is one of purpose: primary taxes fund the day-to-day operations of schools, counties, cities, and community colleges, while secondary taxes fund voter-approved bonds, overrides, and special districts. Since tax year 2015, both are levied on Limited Property Value under Proposition 117.

questions.question_text: Which of the following property tax exemptions exists in Arizona?
questions.correct_answer: Exemptions for veterans and disabled persons
distractors: A universal homeowner's property tax exemption
A senior citizen freeze on all property taxes
An automatic 50% reduction for first-time buyers
questions.explanation: Arizona provides property tax exemptions for veterans, disabled persons, and widows/widowers. Unlike California, Arizona does NOT have a universal homeowner's property tax exemption. Arizona's Senior Property Valuation Protection Option (the "senior freeze," Proposition 104 of 2000) freezes the valuation for qualifying seniors 65+, not the taxes themselves.

questions.question_text: A neighborhood's streets are being repaved through a special improvement district. How are these costs funded?
questions.explanation: Improvement districts fund infrastructure improvements through special assessments charged to properties that benefit from the improvements. This targets costs to the properties that receive the direct benefit.

questions.question_text: Does the Arizona homestead exemption reduce property taxes?
questions.correct_answer: No, it only protects equity from creditors
distractors: Yes, it reduces the assessed value by $25,000
Yes, it exempts the first $50,000 in value
Yes, for primary residence owners only
questions.explanation: The Arizona homestead exemption ($437,600 in 2026, adjusted annually) protects home equity from forced sale by general creditors but is NOT a property tax reduction. It should not be confused with property tax exemptions available to veterans and disabled persons.

questions.question_text: Special assessments for flood control improvements must be approved by:
questions.correct_answer: The affected property owners or governing body
distractors: The Arizona Department of Water Resources
questions.explanation: Special assessments for improvement districts must be approved by the affected property owners or the local governing body. They cannot be imposed unilaterally by a single official.

questions.question_text: Which of the following improvements could be funded through an Arizona improvement district?
questions.correct_answer: Streets, sewers, and flood control systems
questions.explanation: Improvement districts fund public infrastructure improvements like streets, sewers, and flood control that benefit specific areas. Private improvements to individual properties are not eligible.
